project_identification__lead_agency.lead_agency: City of Riverbend Community Development Department

project_location.cross_streets: East Riverbend Boulevard and County Road 12
project_location.within_2_miles_waterways: Feather River; Little Dry Creek; unnamed seasonal drainage along eastern boundary
project_location.within_2_miles_railways: Union Pacific mainline (north-south corridor approx. 1.3 miles west)
project_location.within_2_miles_schools: Riverbend High School; East Riverbend Elementary School; Feather River Community Charter School

development_type.transportation_type: Construction of new collector and local streets, multi-use trails, Class II and Class IV bicycle facilities, and intersection improvements at East Riverbend Boulevard/County Road 12 including new traffic signalization and turn lanes.
development_type.educational_description: One neighborhood-serving elementary school site (approximately 8.5 acres) to be dedicated to the local school district. The site is planned for a two-story K–5 campus with 24 classrooms, a multi-purpose building, administrative offices, outdoor play fields, and shared-use parking that may be available for community events during non-school hours.
development_type.recreational_description: Approximately 17 acres of public parks and recreation facilities, including a 10-acre community park with lighted multi-use sports fields, playgrounds, picnic areas, and a small amphitheater; two neighborhood parks (2–3 acres each) with tot lots and informal turf areas; and an interconnected greenway and trail system along the seasonal drainage corridor with fitness stations and interpretive signage.
development_type.other_development_type_description: Public and quasi-public facilities, including a 2-acre fire station site, stormwater detention and water quality basins (approximately 9 acres total), and an internal transit stop with shelters and bicycle parking to support future local bus service.
development_type.water_facilities_type: On-site potable water distribution system (12-inch and 8-inch mains), fire flow improvements, and two landscaped stormwater detention/water quality basins designed for low-impact development (LID) treatment and peak flow attenuation prior to discharge to the existing seasonal drainage.

project_issues_discussed_in_document.project_issues_other: Energy demand and greenhouse gas emissions associated with building operations and vehicle miles traveled; climate change resilience, including heat island reduction and wildfire smoke exposure considerations.

land_use__project_description.present_land_use_zoning_gp_designation: The project site consists primarily of irrigated orchard and fallow agricultural fields with scattered farm outbuildings and one occupied single-family farmhouse near the northwest corner. Under the City of Riverbend General Plan, the majority of the site is designated "Urban Reserve" with a small portion along East Riverbend Boulevard designated "Community Commercial." Existing zoning in the unincorporated portion of the County is AG-20 (Agriculture, 20-acre minimum) and in the small incorporated portion is AR (Agricultural-Residential). Surrounding land uses include active orchards to the north and east, low-density single-family residential neighborhoods and a middle school to the west across County Road 12, and a mix of rural residences and pastureland to the south.
land_use__project_description.project_description:
The Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Specific Plan ("project") is a comprehensive land use and infrastructure plan to guide the phased development of approximately 185.4 acres on the eastern edge of the City of Riverbend, within the City’s adopted Sphere of Influence. The project is being processed concurrently with a General Plan Amendment, Annexation to the City, Rezone, Subdivision (Tentative Map), Planned Unit Development (PUD), Specific Plan adoption, Site Plan approvals for initial phases, Development Agreement, and associated discretionary permits.

In broad terms, the project would convert predominantly agricultural land into a compact, walkable mixed-use village that integrates a range of housing types, neighborhood-serving commercial uses, employment opportunities, parks and open space, and public facilities. The plan is organized around a central "main street" spine extending south from East Riverbend Boulevard, with higher-intensity mixed-use and commercial uses located near the primary intersection and transitioning to medium- and low-density residential neighborhoods toward the site’s eastern and southern boundaries.

LAND USE PROGRAM

Residential: The project proposes up to 725 dwelling units distributed across several residential neighborhoods and product types:
- Approximately 260 low-density single-family detached units on individual lots (ranging from 4,500 to 7,000 square feet), generally located in the southern and eastern portions of the site.
- Approximately 315 medium-density units consisting of small-lot single-family, duplexes, triplexes, and townhomes clustered around neighborhood parks and along the central greenway.
- Up to 150 higher-density units in two- to three-story garden-style and stacked flats over podium parking located above ground-floor commercial and office uses along the main street corridor.

Commercial and Office: The project includes approximately 145,000 square feet of neighborhood- and community-serving commercial and service uses and 60,000 square feet of office space. Anticipated uses include a small-format grocery store, cafes and restaurants with outdoor seating, neighborhood retail (e.g., pharmacy, dry cleaner, fitness studio), professional and medical offices, and co-working space. Building heights in the commercial core would range from one to three stories, with ground-floor storefronts oriented to the street and upper-story residential or office uses above in select locations.

Public Facilities and Schools: An approximately 8.5-acre elementary school site would be dedicated to the local school district near the center of the project, adjacent to a neighborhood park and within walking distance of the majority of residential units. The plan also identifies a 2-acre site for a future City fire station and public safety facility at the southern edge of the commercial core, sited to allow direct access to County Road 12 and the internal collector street network.

Parks, Open Space, and Recreation: The project would provide a comprehensive system of parks and open space totaling roughly 17 acres of active recreation and an additional 9 acres of stormwater/open space basins that will be landscaped and accessible where feasible. A 10-acre community park in the eastern portion of the site would include multi-use sports fields, a playground, picnic shelters, a half-court basketball area, and a small amphitheater space designed for community events and outdoor movies. Two smaller neighborhood parks (2–3 acres each) would provide tot lots, informal turf play areas, shade trees, and seating. A greenway corridor would follow the existing seasonal drainage, combining habitat enhancement, multi-use trails, and water quality features.

Transportation and Circulation: The project would construct a network of new public streets, including one primary north-south collector connecting East Riverbend Boulevard to the southern boundary of the site, two secondary east-west collectors, and a series of local residential streets with traffic calming features. The East Riverbend Boulevard/County Road 12 intersection would be improved with new turn lanes, signalization, pedestrian crosswalks, and bicycle facilities. The internal circulation plan emphasizes complete streets, with sidewalks on both sides of all public streets, Class II bike lanes on collector streets, and a Class I multi-use path along the greenway corridor. Space for a future local bus stop with shelters and bike racks is reserved near the commercial core and elementary school.

Infrastructure and Utilities: The project would extend existing City water, sewer, and recycled water lines from existing mains in County Road 12 and East Riverbend Boulevard. On-site potable water distribution would be provided via 12-inch and 8-inch mains with fire hydrants meeting City and fire district standards. Wastewater would be conveyed by gravity to a new trunk line in County Road 12, ultimately discharging to the City’s regional wastewater treatment facility; no on-site package plant is proposed. Stormwater management would incorporate low-impact development (LID) features such as bioretention planters, vegetated swales, and two primary detention/water quality basins sized to maintain post-development peak flows at or below existing conditions for the 10- and 100-year storm events. Dry utilities (electric, gas, telecommunications) would be installed underground throughout the project.

Phasing: Development is anticipated to occur in four major phases over an approximate 10- to 15-year buildout horizon, depending on market conditions. Initial phases would focus on the northern portion of the site closest to existing infrastructure, including a portion of the commercial main street, the first residential neighborhoods, and interim stormwater facilities. Later phases would extend improvements south and east, complete the community park and greenway system, and build out the remaining residential and commercial areas. The Specific Plan includes a phasing plan tying infrastructure improvements, school and park land dedications, and traffic mitigation measures to defined development thresholds.

Sustainability and Design Features: The project incorporates a variety of sustainability-oriented measures, including a compact land use pattern that supports walking and biking, electric vehicle (EV) charging readiness in residential garages and commercial parking lots, solar-ready building design, extensive street tree planting for shade and heat island reduction, and drought-tolerant landscaping with efficient irrigation. Architectural guidelines emphasize human-scaled building frontages, porches and stoops along residential streets, and active ground-floor uses in the commercial core. Design standards address building massing, materials, signage, lighting, and fencing to ensure a cohesive village character.

Overall, the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Specific Plan is intended to provide a balanced mix of housing, employment, and services in a walkable pattern, while carefully managing transitions to adjacent agricultural and rural residential areas, protecting and enhancing the existing seasonal drainage corridor, and implementing infrastructure improvements that are consistent with the City’s adopted General Plan policies and regional growth strategies.
